description: The device provides up to 32 general-purpose pins that can be configured individually as inputs or outputs. Software can read inputs and drive outputs using memory-mapped GPIO registers. Atomic set/clear registers avoid read-modify-write hazards during output updates. Input sampling is synchronized to the module clock for stability. Correct operation requires enabling the GPIO clock and selecting the GPIO function in pinmux before use.
evidence: Device data manual SPRS371 – GPIO chapter; Registers: OUTDATA, INDATA, SET_DATA, CLR_DATA
Remarks: Enable pinmux and PSC before accessing GPIO registers; use atomic SET_/CLR_ writes for outputs.

description: All GPIO inputs pass through synchronizing flip-flops to reduce metastability when sampling asynchronous signals. This introduces a small latency from pin to INDATA visibility. The synchronizers operate on the enabled GPIO module clock. No configuration is typically required beyond clock enable. Designers should account for one or more cycles of sampling delay in fast handshake paths.
evidence: Device data manual SPRS371 – GPIO input path description
Remarks: Use double-sampled INDATA reads if glitch filtering is needed at the software level.

description: Per-pin edge detection logic is available to monitor rising and/or falling transitions. The logic can generate interrupt or status events when enabled. Configuration is done via dedicated trigger enable/disable registers. Edge detection operates on synchronized inputs to ensure stable event capture. Clearing status after service is necessary to avoid retrigger.
evidence: Registers: SET_RIS_TRIG, CLR_RIS_TRIG, SET_FAL_TRIG, CLR_FAL_TRIG, INSTAT
Remarks: Configure only one polarity if spurious toggles are expected; always clear status in ISRs.

description: GPIO pins are multiplexed with other peripheral functions and must be selected via pinmux settings. Reset straps and software-programmable pinmux registers determine which function is active on each package pin. Before using any GP[n] as GPIO, the pinmux must map that pin to the GPIO function. Incorrect pinmux configuration will make GPIO reads/writes ineffective. Package and ballout options impact availability of specific GPIOs.
evidence: Device pinmux registers; Device data manual SPRS371 – Pin multiplexing chapter
Remarks: Apply pinmux configuration early in boot and lock it if accidental reconfiguration is a risk.

description: The Power and Sleep Controller (PSC) gates the clock to the GPIO module by default. Software must enable the local PSC (LPSC) entry for GPIO to supply the functional clock. Without clock enable, register accesses may read as inactive and signals will not update. Transition to the appropriate PSC state machine state before configuration. Proper sequencing avoids partial writes during clock off conditions.
evidence: PSC/LPSC for GPIO; Device data manual SPRS371 – Power/Clock gating
Remarks: Enable PSC for GPIO prior to any register access and disable for power savings when idle.

description: Once enabled, the GPIO module clock typically runs at CPU/6. This relationship impacts input sampling latency and output toggle rates. Timing of edge detection and interrupt capture is also based on this clock. Software should consider this divisor when designing high-frequency IO control loops. Actual ratios may vary with device clocking configuration but the nominal is CPU/6 as documented.
evidence: Device data manual SPRS371 – GPIO clocking description
Remarks: Account for CPU/6 rate when estimating maximum GPIO toggle or polling rates.

description: The DIR register provides per-pin selection of input or output for GP[31:0]. Writes update the direction latch and determine whether subsequent data writes affect the pin. Changing direction should be coordinated with output initialization to avoid unintended pulses. Reading the register returns the current direction configuration. Reserved bits must be preserved on writes.
evidence: Register: DIR; Device data manual SPRS371 – GPIO registers
Remarks: Write DIR after initializing output levels using SET_/CLR_ to avoid glitches when switching to output.

description: SET_DATA provides an atomic method to drive selected GPIO outputs high. It eliminates read-modify-write hazards that can occur with port-wide writes. Each bit written as 1 sets the corresponding output latch; zeros have no effect. This is preferred in interrupt contexts to avoid race conditions. Use with CLR_DATA to control outputs deterministically.
evidence: Register: SET_DATA; Device data manual SPRS371 – GPIO registers
Remarks: Favor SET_DATA over OUTDATA for concurrency safety during set operations.

description: CLR_DATA provides an atomic method to drive selected GPIO outputs low. Like SET_DATA, each bit written as 1 clears the corresponding output latch. This avoids read-modify-write issues and is safe under interrupt or multi-context access. Behavior on writing zeros is no change to other bits. Pair with SET_DATA for robust output manipulation.
evidence: Register: CLR_DATA; Device data manual SPRS371 – GPIO registers
Remarks: Favor CLR_DATA over OUTDATA for concurrency safety during clear operations.

description: OUTDATA allows port-wide write and readback of the latched output state. While convenient, port-wide writes can suffer RMW hazards if not protected. Reading OUTDATA returns the current output latch state for all pins configured as outputs. Software should consider atomic registers for bit updates. Use OUTDATA primarily for initialization or bulk updates under protection.
evidence: Register: OUTDATA; Device data manual SPRS371 – GPIO registers
Remarks: Avoid OUTDATA for single-bit toggles in ISR unless exclusive access is guaranteed.

description: INDATA provides synchronized readback of per-pin input levels. The values reflect the state after input synchronizers and the module clock domain. Polling INDATA is suitable for simple input monitoring. For event-driven designs, combine with edge-detect and interrupts. Ensure the GPIO clock is enabled to obtain valid samples.
evidence: Register: INDATA; Device data manual SPRS371 – GPIO registers
Remarks: Debounce in software if external signals are noisy or lack hardware filtering.

description: This register enables rising-edge detection per pin using atomic set semantics. Writing ones enables detection for the selected pins while preserving others. Combined with interrupt routing, this allows event-driven input handling. Clearing pending status is necessary before enabling to avoid stale triggers. Use paired clear register to disable when needed.
evidence: Register: SET_RIS_TRIG; Device data manual SPRS371 – GPIO interrupt control
Remarks: Initialize by clearing INSTAT, then program SET_RIS_TRIG to prevent spurious initial interrupts.

description: This register disables rising-edge detection per pin using atomic clear semantics. It allows selective deactivation without disturbing other configuration. Disabling may be used during critical sections to suppress interrupts. The register affects only rising-edge configuration bits. Re-enable later via SET_RIS_TRIG as required.
evidence: Register: CLR_RIS_TRIG; Device data manual SPRS371 – GPIO interrupt control
Remarks: Use to temporarily mask rising-edge sources during reconfiguration or ISR execution.

description: This register enables falling-edge detection per pin using atomic set semantics. It supports detection of negative transitions on inputs. When combined with rising-edge enable, both-edge detection is possible. Status must be serviced to avoid repeated interrupts. Enable only the needed edges to minimize noise sensitivity.
evidence: Register: SET_FAL_TRIG; Device data manual SPRS371 – GPIO interrupt control
Remarks: Prefer single-edge enable unless both-edge semantics are explicitly required.

description: This register disables falling-edge detection per pin using atomic clear semantics. It provides granular control over interrupt sensitivity. Use it to quiesce inputs during transitions or maintenance modes. Other edge settings remain unaffected by these writes. Re-enable via the corresponding set register when needed.
evidence: Register: CLR_FAL_TRIG; Device data manual SPRS371 – GPIO interrupt control
Remarks: Apply during safe windows to avoid missing intended events.

description: INSTAT indicates which pins have pending edge events. Software must read this register in the ISR to identify sources. Clearing the status after handling prevents retrigger. Status bits correlate with the enabled edge-detect configuration. Proper ordering of reads and clears avoids race conditions.
evidence: Register: INSTAT; Device data manual SPRS371 – GPIO interrupt status
Remarks: Read INSTAT early in the ISR and clear all serviced bits before exiting.

description: INTSTAT01 provides bank-level aggregation status for GPIO interrupts. It helps quickly determine if any GPIO in a bank triggered an interrupt. Software uses this to narrow down the source before checking per-pin status. Clearing requires servicing the underlying pin-level conditions. Integration with the CPU interrupt controller is required for routing.
evidence: Register: INTSTAT01; Device data manual SPRS371 – GPIO bank interrupt status
Remarks: Use bank status first to reduce per-pin reads in large ISR fan-in scenarios.

description: Individual GPIO lines can be routed directly as CPU interrupts. This allows dedicated, low-latency servicing for critical signals. Configuration is performed in the CPU interrupt controller rather than the GPIO module. GPIO status must still be read and cleared in the GPIO block. Use sparingly due to limited CPU interrupt inputs.
evidence: CPU Interrupt Controller (INTC) configuration; Device data manual SPRS371 – Interrupts
Remarks: Reserve individual lines for latency-critical events; others may use banked interrupts.

description: GPIOs can be ORed to a bank-level interrupt presented to the CPU. This reduces interrupt input usage at the expense of additional software demultiplexing. Software reads INTSTAT01 and per-pin status to identify the source. Proper clearing of all contributing sources is needed before exit. This approach scales for many GPIO inputs.
evidence: Register: INTSTAT01; CPU INTC routing; Device data manual SPRS371 – Interrupts
Remarks: Adopt banked mode for high fan-in designs to preserve CPU interrupt lines.

description: Per-pin configuration allows rising, falling, or both edges to act as interrupt events. This enables flexible response to external signal semantics. Configuration is done via dedicated trigger control registers. Both-edge operation increases sensitivity to noise and should be used judiciously. Always clear stale status before enabling.
evidence: Registers: SET_RIS_TRIG, CLR_RIS_TRIG, SET_FAL_TRIG, CLR_FAL_TRIG
Remarks: Document edge policy per input signal and validate with scope-based bring-up.

description: Interrupt service routines must acknowledge and clear all pending GPIO events. Failure to clear can cause immediate retrigger or lost events. The order of reading status and clearing should prevent races with new edges. If using banked interrupts, both bank and pin-level sources must be serviced. Consider minimal ISR work and defer processing to tasks.
evidence: Registers: INSTAT, INTSTAT01; CPU interrupt flag handling
Remarks: Design ISRs to be fast, clear-first, and re-entrant-safe with atomic operations.

description: GPIO-generated interrupts can be used to trigger EDMA transfers. This enables hardware-driven data movement in response to external events. Proper event mapping is required in the EDMA controller. GPIO only provides the event; EDMA performs the transfer. Ensure latency and priority settings meet throughput needs.
evidence: EDMA event mapping; Device data manual SPRS371 – EDMA/interrupt integration
Remarks: Use for sporadic or burst transfers initiated by external GPIO activity.

description: A recommended sequence is: configure pinmux, enable PSC clock, set directions, initialize outputs, program edge triggers, and clear pending status. Finally, enable routing in the CPU/EDMA controllers as needed. This ordering prevents glitches and spurious interrupts. It also ensures registers are written only when clocks are active. Following a consistent init template improves bring-up reliability.
evidence: Pinmux settings; PSC LPSC enable; DIR/SET_/CLR_; INSTAT/INTSTAT01; INTC/EDMA routing
Remarks: Automate the sequence and add assertions for clock enabled and status cleared.

description: Writes to registers must preserve reserved bits by writing only documented fields. Overwriting reserved bits can lead to undefined behavior. Read-modify-write with masks prevents unintended changes. Reference the register descriptions to determine valid bitfields. This practice applies to all GPIO registers and related control blocks.
evidence: Device data manual SPRS371 – Register field descriptions
Remarks: Always use defined masks and verify post-write readback when possible.

description: Dedicated SET_ and CLR_ registers provide atomic per-bit updates. These operations avoid races in interrupt or multi-core scenarios. They are preferred for concurrent software access patterns. Using them simplifies ISR design and eliminates the need for locks. OUTDATA remains useful for bulk writes under protection.
evidence: Registers: SET_DATA, CLR_DATA; Device data manual SPRS371 – GPIO registers
Remarks: Adopt SET_/CLR_ for all single-bit changes to guarantee atomicity.

description: GPIOs are arranged into banks for efficient interrupt aggregation. Bank status registers summarize activity across a group of pins. Software uses bank information to quickly determine if detailed per-pin inspection is needed. This organization helps scale systems with many GPIOs. Knowledge of bank mapping informs ISR demultiplexing logic.
evidence: Register: INTSTAT01; Device data manual SPRS371 – GPIO bank structure
Remarks: Design ISR flow to check bank status first, then drill down to pin-level reads.

description: The device supports using either individual GPIO interrupts, banked interrupts, or both. This flexibility allows balancing latency against CPU interrupt resource usage. Configuration is performed through the CPU interrupt controller and GPIO status handling. Software must ensure consistent clearing when both paths are enabled. Selection should match application criticality and fan-in needs.
evidence: CPU INTC configuration; GPIO status/clear registers; Device data manual SPRS371 – Interrupt routing
Remarks: Document routing choices and verify no duplicate servicing when dual paths are enabled.

description: Availability of specific GP[n] signals depends on package option and pinmux selection. Not all GPIOs may be bonded out on every device/package. The data manual provides the definitive mapping and availability. Software must not assume continuous numbering without checking. Incorrect assumptions can lead to non-functional IOs.
evidence: Device data manual SPRS371 – Package/pin tables; Pinmux chapter
Remarks: Cross-check schematic and data manual tables before assigning GPIO functions.

description: The PSC can gate the GPIO module when not in use to reduce power. Transitions must follow the PSC state machine rules to avoid glitches. Before gating off, ensure outputs are in a safe state and interrupts are disabled. Upon re-enabling, reinitialize any volatile configuration if needed. This enables dynamic power management for IO subsystems.
evidence: PSC/LPSC procedures; Device data manual SPRS371 – Power management
Remarks: Integrate PSC transitions with system idle policies and ISR masking.

description: Using GPIO to trigger EDMA requires programming EDMA channel and event mapping. The configuration resides entirely in the EDMA3 controller and related crossbar if present. GPIO provides the event source but does not manage transfer parameters. Proper synchronization and completion handling are required. Validate transfer sizes and priorities under expected burst conditions.
evidence: EDMA3 controller configuration; Device data manual SPRS371 – EDMA event mapping
Remarks: Preallocate channels and use consistent event-to-channel mappings across the system.

description: JTAG/emulation behavior for GPIO is not detailed in the provided excerpt. No special driver behavior is required based on this information. Standard debug access should be safe when clocks are enabled. Interactions with halt states may impact IO timing but are device-tool dependent. Further guidance requires consulting dedicated emulation documentation.
evidence: UNKNOWN
Remarks: Assume normal operation; consult emulation manuals for halt/resume effects on GPIO.
